text: Typed holes are great!  We need more paraconsistency in our lives.
Typed holes are great!  We need more paraconsistency in our lives.
I haven't seen Hazel before, but it actually looks really exciting. Structured editing and live programming with a formal semantics? Sounds like it could bring on a revolution.
I haven't seen Hazel before, but it actually looks really exciting. Structured editing and live programming with a formal semantics? Sounds like it could bring on a revolution.
Also the language is based on Elm, which is an amazing starting point.

<@UC25EFSJC>
<@UC25EFSJC>
hihi, happy to chat with anyone about hazel!
hihi, happy to chat with anyone about hazel!

Hey Adam, glad that you joined!

What kind of program synthesis are you interested in? Could you tell more about it?
Hah, of course you’re here.
Hah, of course you’re here.

Thanks!  Well, I'm new to the field, so in part I'm just gathering impressions from what others have done.  I caught Nadia Polikarpova and James Koppel's *great* talks at Strange Loop this week.  I'm also tinkering with building my own synthesis system from the ground up, starting with a very minimal reversible machine.  My hunch is that there ought to be an advantage in enumerative and search aspects of the problem if the search space has a nice algebraic structure.

root.text: Hi folks!  I've been a developer for a couple decades and have been doing research mathematics in finite group theory and art hacks on the side for some time.  I'm currently shifting my career away from commercial software development and pursuing my own research harder.  At the moment obsessed with program synthesis...which is how I found this forum.

text: Have you heard of version space algebras?

<@UC25EFSJC> I have a question :stuck_out_tongue:. How does Hazel compare to other functional structure editors like Lamdu? Is it just about formalizing program holes, or would Hazel also lead to substantially new editor capabilities? And also, do you think that structure editors need usability improvements before they have a chance at being widely adopted? Is that part of the Hazel project?

root.text: I was pretty excited about this project: <http://hazel.org/>

attachments.fallback: Hazel, a live functional programming environment featuring typed holes.

text: Hazel and Lamdu are definitely quite similar. So far, the Hazel project has been more focused on theoretical foundations and Lamdu has a much more developed UI. The Lamdu UI is much more "experimental" -- with Hazel, we're aiming for something that feels mostly like text except when text doesn't make sense.

Hazel and Lamdu are definitely quite similar. So far, the Hazel project has been more focused on theoretical foundations and Lamdu has a much more developed UI. The Lamdu UI is much more "experimental" -- with Hazel, we're aiming for something that feels mostly like text except when text doesn't make sense.
Hazel has a different approach for running programs with holes and live programming.
Hazel has a different approach for running programs with holes and live programming.

Briefly, Lamdu stops at holes and tracks all function applications, whereas Hazel continues past holes and tracks closures only around holes. There is more on that on our draft paper at <http://hazel.org|hazel.org>.

Briefly, Lamdu stops at holes and tracks all function applications, whereas Hazel continues past holes and tracks closures only around holes. There is more on that on our draft paper at <http://hazel.org|hazel.org>.

Going forward, we're definitely going to be doing more work on scaling Hazel up and experimenting with the UI. There is definitely a lot more left to do on the UI side. I think we're gonna keep exploring the "mostly text-like" ("hybrid") structure editing approach.

Going forward, we're definitely going to be doing more work on scaling Hazel up and experimenting with the UI. There is definitely a lot more left to do on the UI side. I think we're gonna keep exploring the "mostly text-like" ("hybrid") structure editing approach.
I don't know if it will be practical or not long-term. I think there has been some reason to believe it can be with recent empirical work with mbeddr in particular.
I don't know if it will be practical or not long-term. I think there has been some reason to believe it can be with recent empirical work with mbeddr in particular.

Awesome, I hope it goes well :slightly_smiling_face:. I'm looking forward to seeing the new editing experiences you can enable for an Elm-like language. IMO the kind of work you're doing is an important part of the programming environments puzzle.

MPS/mbeddr definitely looks like it can provide a nicer (saner) editing experience than lamdu.

I have not heard of those.  Looks interesting, thanks for the lead!
If you want a program synth reading list, or have any other questions, just let me know.
I disagree with this point: "Atom, Sublime Edit, and Visual Studio Code are neat, but they do not represent a great improvement over TextMate circa 2007"
TextMate lacks important customization APIs and correspondingly is missing table-stakes-for-2018 features the rest of those editors have: live autocomplete, splits, and linter integrations.
Note I prefer TextMate to all the rest of those editors, but claiming they haven't advanced programming since 2007 TextMate is false.
The paragraph after has a bunch of great points though
